items.passage: Entropy is a measure of the number of microscopic arrangements that correspond to a system's observable state, and it tends to increase as energy spreads out. A gas confined to one corner of a container occupies few arrangements, but once released it disperses until molecules fill the available space evenly. This dispersal is not driven by any force pushing the molecules apart. [■] Instead, it reflects probability: there are vastly more arrangements in which the gas is spread out than ones in which it is bunched together. [■] The system drifts toward the more probable condition simply because such states are overwhelmingly numerous. [■] This statistical tendency explains why heat flows from hot bodies to cold ones rather than the reverse. [■] When the two are joined, energy redistributes until both reach a common temperature, the arrangement of greatest probability. The principle reaches far beyond gases. It sets a direction for time itself, since processes that increase total entropy proceed while their reverse almost never occurs. However, the law is statistical rather than absolute, and over extremely long intervals rare fluctuations toward lower entropy remain conceivable, though they are never observed in practice.
paragraphs: Entropy is a measure of the number of microscopic arrangements that correspond to a system's observable state, and it tends to increase as energy spreads out. A gas confined to one corner of a container occupies few arrangements, but once released it disperses until molecules fill the available space evenly. This dispersal is not driven by any force pushing the molecules apart. [■]

Instead, it reflects probability: there are vastly more arrangements in which the gas is spread out than ones in which it is bunched together. [■] The system drifts toward the more probable condition simply because such states are overwhelmingly numerous. [■] This statistical tendency explains why heat flows from hot bodies to cold ones rather than the reverse. [■] When the two are joined, energy redistributes until both reach a common temperature, the arrangement of greatest probability.

The principle reaches far beyond gases. It sets a direction for time itself, since processes that increase total entropy proceed while their reverse almost never occurs. However, the law is statistical rather than absolute, and over extremely long intervals rare fluctuations toward lower entropy remain conceivable, though they are never observed in practice.

questions.stem: The word "disperses" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: condenses
options.B: scatters
options.C: freezes
options.D: vibrates

questions.explanation: The gas "disperses until molecules fill the available space evenly," meaning it scatters throughout the container.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why does a released gas spread out to fill its container?

options.A: Because a force pushes its molecules apart
options.B: Because spread-out arrangements are far more numerous
options.C: Because cold air draws the molecules outward
options.D: Because the container heats the molecules unevenly

questions.explanation: The passage says dispersal "is not driven by any force" but reflects that "there are vastly more arrangements in which the gas is spread out."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about heat flowing from a hot body to a cold one?

options.A: It requires an external force to begin
options.B: It occurs only when temperatures are already equal
options.C: It moves the system toward a more probable arrangement
options.D: It reverses direction once the bodies are joined

questions.explanation: Heat flow is described as a "statistical tendency" ending at "the arrangement of greatest probability," so it moves the system toward a more probable state.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention the flow of heat from hot bodies to cold ones in paragraph 2?

options.A: To show that the same statistical tendency governs familiar processes
options.B: To prove that heat can never move between objects
options.C: To argue that entropy decreases when objects touch
options.D: To describe how a single gas molecule behaves

questions.explanation: The heat example follows "This statistical tendency explains," illustrating that the probability principle governs everyday heat flow.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, both a fast-moving and a slow-moving collection of molecules count as a single temperature, yet countless distinct arrangements produce it.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 (paragraph 1, after 'pushing the molecules apart.')
options.B: Slot 2 (paragraph 2, after 'bunched together.')
options.C: Slot 3 (paragraph 2, after 'overwhelmingly numerous.')
options.D: Slot 4 (paragraph 2, after 'hot bodies to cold ones rather than the reverse.')

questions.explanation: The example linking many arrangements to a single temperature best follows the sentence introducing heat flow and temperature at slot 4, elaborating that point.

items.passage: Regenerated cellulose fibers are textile threads made by dissolving natural plant cellulose and reforming it into long, continuous filaments. The earliest attempts, in the late nineteenth century, produced a fiber often called artificial silk. [■] Inventors pressed a thick cellulose solution through tiny holes into a bath that hardened the strands, yielding a glossy thread. Yet these first fibers were dangerously flammable and weakened sharply when wet, limiting their use. [■] A refined process soon overcame the worst of these flaws. By treating cellulose with new chemicals before spinning, manufacturers produced a more stable filament that resisted moisture far better than its predecessor. [■] This intermediate fiber, marketed as viscose, became cheap enough for everyday clothing and dominated the market for decades. [■] Modern methods have pushed the idea further still. Using recyclable solvents, factories now dissolve wood pulp and spin it into strong fibers with little chemical waste, an approach prized for its lower environmental cost. The lasting impact is a family of fabrics derived from renewable plants rather than petroleum. However, the energy demanded by dissolving and reforming cellulose remains high, and critics note that the label natural can obscure an intensively industrial origin.
paragraphs: Regenerated cellulose fibers are textile threads made by dissolving natural plant cellulose and reforming it into long, continuous filaments. The earliest attempts, in the late nineteenth century, produced a fiber often called artificial silk. [■] Inventors pressed a thick cellulose solution through tiny holes into a bath that hardened the strands, yielding a glossy thread. Yet these first fibers were dangerously flammable and weakened sharply when wet, limiting their use. [■]

A refined process soon overcame the worst of these flaws. By treating cellulose with new chemicals before spinning, manufacturers produced a more stable filament that resisted moisture far better than its predecessor. [■] This intermediate fiber, marketed as viscose, became cheap enough for everyday clothing and dominated the market for decades. [■]

Modern methods have pushed the idea further still. Using recyclable solvents, factories now dissolve wood pulp and spin it into strong fibers with little chemical waste, an approach prized for its lower environmental cost. The lasting impact is a family of fabrics derived from renewable plants rather than petroleum. However, the energy demanded by dissolving and reforming cellulose remains high, and critics note that the label natural can obscure an intensively industrial origin.

questions.stem: The word "hardened" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: solidified
options.B: colored
options.C: loosened
options.D: measured

questions.explanation: The bath "hardened the strands" into a thread, meaning it caused the soft solution to solidify.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what was a serious drawback of the earliest regenerated cellulose fibers?

options.A: They could not be dyed in bright colors
options.B: They were too costly for ordinary clothing
options.C: They were highly flammable and weak when wet
options.D: They required petroleum as a raw material

questions.explanation: The passage states the first fibers "were dangerously flammable and weakened sharply when wet."

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true of viscose EXCEPT

options.A: It resisted moisture better than the earliest fiber
options.B: It was spun using recyclable solvents and little waste
options.C: It became cheap enough for everyday clothing
options.D: It dominated the textile market for decades

questions.explanation: Recyclable solvents and little waste describe modern methods in paragraph 3, not viscose, which is the intermediate fiber of paragraph 2.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about modern regenerated fibers compared with earlier ones?

options.A: They are weaker than the earliest artificial silk
options.B: They are made entirely without dissolving cellulose
options.C: They have abandoned plant material in favor of petroleum
options.D: They are produced with less harm to the environment

questions.explanation: Modern methods use "recyclable solvents" with "little chemical waste" and "lower environmental cost," implying less environmental harm than earlier processes.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'And because it was so inexpensive, this fiber soon appeared in dresses, linings, and household fabrics across the world.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 (paragraph 1, after 'artificial silk.')
options.B: Slot 2 (paragraph 1, after 'limiting their use.')
options.C: Slot 3 (paragraph 2, after 'than its predecessor.')
options.D: Slot 4 (paragraph 2, after 'dominated the market for decades.')

questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates on the cheap, widely used viscose fiber, so it best follows the statement that viscose was cheap and dominated the market, at slot 4.

items.passage: Domestication is the gradual process by which humans reshape wild plants and animals into forms better suited to cultivation and care. It begins when people repeatedly favor individuals with useful traits, such as larger seeds or a calmer temperament, allowing those traits to spread across generations. [■] Over time the selected populations diverge from their wild ancestors, depending on human attention to survive. The relationship is mutual, since the people who tend these species reshape their own way of life as well. In cereals, domestication left clear physical marks. Wild grasses scatter their seeds the moment they ripen, but plants whose seeds cling to the stalk are far easier to gather. [■] Harvesting by hand unintentionally favored these non-shattering forms, and within centuries fields were dominated by grain that waited to be collected. [■] Larger kernels and tougher husks followed the same logic of repeated selection. [■] The significance of domestication extends well beyond the farm. It allowed dense, settled communities to form, supported by predictable harvests rather than the search for wild food. However, this dependence carried risks, for populations tied to a few crops became newly vulnerable to drought and disease.
paragraphs: Domestication is the gradual process by which humans reshape wild plants and animals into forms better suited to cultivation and care. It begins when people repeatedly favor individuals with useful traits, such as larger seeds or a calmer temperament, allowing those traits to spread across generations. [■] Over time the selected populations diverge from their wild ancestors, depending on human attention to survive. The relationship is mutual, since the people who tend these species reshape their own way of life as well.

In cereals, domestication left clear physical marks. Wild grasses scatter their seeds the moment they ripen, but plants whose seeds cling to the stalk are far easier to gather. [■] Harvesting by hand unintentionally favored these non-shattering forms, and within centuries fields were dominated by grain that waited to be collected. [■] Larger kernels and tougher husks followed the same logic of repeated selection. [■]

The significance of domestication extends well beyond the farm. It allowed dense, settled communities to form, supported by predictable harvests rather than the search for wild food. However, this dependence carried risks, for populations tied to a few crops became newly vulnerable to drought and disease.

questions.stem: The word "diverge" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: compete
options.B: differ
options.C: recover
options.D: migrate

questions.explanation: The selected populations "diverge from their wild ancestors," meaning they grow to differ from them.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why did non-shattering cereals come to dominate early fields?

options.A: Because farmers deliberately bred them for taste
options.B: Because hand-harvesting unintentionally favored seeds that clung to the stalk
options.C: Because their wild ancestors had already disappeared
options.D: Because they ripened far earlier than other grasses

questions.explanation: The passage says "harvesting by hand unintentionally favored these non-shattering forms," whose seeds cling to the stalk.

questions.stem: What does "those traits" refer to in paragraph 1?

options.A: wild plants and animals
options.B: settled communities
options.C: human attention and care
options.D: useful traits such as larger seeds

questions.explanation: "Those traits" refers back to the "useful traits, such as larger seeds or a calmer temperament" that people favor.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about settled farming communities?

options.A: They abandoned agriculture during droughts
options.B: They relied less on plants than on wild animals
options.C: Their stability came with a new fragility
options.D: They were smaller than groups that gathered wild food

questions.explanation: Settled communities formed on predictable harvests yet "became newly vulnerable to drought and disease," implying their stability brought new fragility.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For instance, a herder who spares the gentlest sheep each season slowly produces a flock that tolerates close human contact.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 (paragraph 1, after 'spread across generations.')
options.B: Slot 2 (paragraph 2, after 'far easier to gather.')
options.C: Slot 3 (paragraph 2, after 'grain that waited to be collected.')
options.D: Slot 4 (paragraph 2, after 'logic of repeated selection.')

questions.explanation: The herder example illustrates favoring useful traits like a calmer temperament across generations, so it best follows that general statement at slot 1.

items.passage: Permanent magnets are materials that retain a magnetic field without any continuous supply of electric current, making them essential to motors and generators. The earliest such magnets were simple iron alloys, weak and easily demagnetized by heat or a sharp blow. [■] Engineers compensated by using large, heavy pieces, which made early electric devices bulky and inefficient. The limitation slowed the spread of compact machinery for decades. [■] A major advance came with alloys that combined iron with elements such as aluminum, nickel, and cobalt. These mixtures held their magnetism far more firmly and tolerated higher temperatures, allowing smaller and more reliable components. [■] Yet they still required considerable volume to deliver strong fields. The decisive breakthrough arrived with magnets built from rare-earth elements. These store enormous magnetic energy in a tiny mass, enabling the slim motors found in modern vehicles, hard drives, and wind turbines. [■] Their lasting impact is visible wherever powerful force must fit a small space. However, the supply of rare-earth elements is concentrated in a few regions, and their extraction is environmentally costly, leaving manufacturers exposed to disruptions beyond their control.
paragraphs: Permanent magnets are materials that retain a magnetic field without any continuous supply of electric current, making them essential to motors and generators. The earliest such magnets were simple iron alloys, weak and easily demagnetized by heat or a sharp blow. [■] Engineers compensated by using large, heavy pieces, which made early electric devices bulky and inefficient. The limitation slowed the spread of compact machinery for decades. [■]

A major advance came with alloys that combined iron with elements such as aluminum, nickel, and cobalt. These mixtures held their magnetism far more firmly and tolerated higher temperatures, allowing smaller and more reliable components. [■] Yet they still required considerable volume to deliver strong fields.

The decisive breakthrough arrived with magnets built from rare-earth elements. These store enormous magnetic energy in a tiny mass, enabling the slim motors found in modern vehicles, hard drives, and wind turbines. [■] Their lasting impact is visible wherever powerful force must fit a small space. However, the supply of rare-earth elements is concentrated in a few regions, and their extraction is environmentally costly, leaving manufacturers exposed to disruptions beyond their control.

questions.stem: The word "retain" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: keep
options.B: release
options.C: weaken
options.D: transfer

questions.explanation: Permanent magnets "retain a magnetic field without any continuous supply of electric current," meaning they keep it.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why were early electric devices bulky and inefficient?

options.A: Because rare-earth elements were too expensive
options.B: Because they relied on a continuous electric current
options.C: Because aluminum and nickel were difficult to obtain
options.D: Because engineers compensated for weak magnets with large, heavy pieces

questions.explanation: The passage says engineers "compensated by using large, heavy pieces, which made early electric devices bulky and inefficient."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about rare-earth magnets?

options.A: They demagnetize more easily than iron alloys
options.B: They cannot tolerate high temperatures
options.C: They are no longer used in modern motors
options.D: They make compact, powerful devices possible

questions.explanation: Because they "store enormous magnetic energy in a tiny mass," enabling "slim motors," rare-earth magnets allow compact yet powerful devices.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention vehicles, hard drives, and wind turbines in paragraph 3?

options.A: To argue that these devices no longer use magnets
options.B: To explain how rare-earth elements are mined
options.C: To illustrate the wide reach of rare-earth magnets
options.D: To show why iron alloys remain the strongest magnets

questions.explanation: These examples follow the claim about slim motors, illustrating how broadly rare-earth magnets are applied.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT

options.A: Iron-aluminum-nickel-cobalt alloys tolerated higher temperatures than simple iron
options.B: Rare-earth elements are abundant and evenly distributed worldwide
options.C: The earliest iron magnets could be demagnetized by heat or a blow
options.D: Rare-earth magnets store great magnetic energy in a small mass

questions.explanation: The passage states the rare-earth supply "is concentrated in a few regions," contradicting the claim that it is abundant and evenly distributed.

items.passage: Reconstructing the timing of past ice ages long posed a serious challenge, since the glaciers that carved the landscape vanished tens of thousands of years ago. Surface clues such as scratched bedrock and heaps of transported rock revealed that ice had advanced and retreated repeatedly, but they offered no reliable clock. Without a way to date these events, scientists could not test whether the cycles followed any regular rhythm. The breakthrough came from sediment drawn out of the deep ocean floor. Tiny shells buried in these cores preserve a chemical record of past temperature, and because the layers accumulate steadily, their depth corresponds to age. By analyzing the shells layer by layer, researchers reconstructed a continuous history of cooling and warming stretching back over a million years. This record revealed a striking pattern. The ice ages recurred at regular intervals that matched slow, predictable changes in the shape of Earth's orbit and the tilt of its axis. The match confirmed that astronomical cycles pace the glaciers. However, the orbital changes alone are too faint to drive such large swings, so feedbacks within the climate must amplify them, a mechanism still incompletely understood.
paragraphs: Reconstructing the timing of past ice ages long posed a serious challenge, since the glaciers that carved the landscape vanished tens of thousands of years ago. Surface clues such as scratched bedrock and heaps of transported rock revealed that ice had advanced and retreated repeatedly, but they offered no reliable clock. Without a way to date these events, scientists could not test whether the cycles followed any regular rhythm.

The breakthrough came from sediment drawn out of the deep ocean floor. Tiny shells buried in these cores preserve a chemical record of past temperature, and because the layers accumulate steadily, their depth corresponds to age. By analyzing the shells layer by layer, researchers reconstructed a continuous history of cooling and warming stretching back over a million years.

This record revealed a striking pattern. The ice ages recurred at regular intervals that matched slow, predictable changes in the shape of Earth's orbit and the tilt of its axis. The match confirmed that astronomical cycles pace the glaciers. However, the orbital changes alone are too faint to drive such large swings, so feedbacks within the climate must amplify them, a mechanism still incompletely understood.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: How ocean sediments helped reveal the regular timing of ice ages
options.B: How glaciers physically carve scratches into exposed bedrock
options.C: Why tiny shells form on the deep ocean floor
options.D: How changes in Earth's orbit are measured today

questions.explanation: The passage sets out the dating problem, the sediment-core solution, and the orbital pattern it revealed, making ocean-sediment dating of ice-age timing its full scope.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why does the depth of a sediment layer correspond to its age?

options.A: Because shells dissolve at a fixed rate
options.B: Because the layers accumulate steadily over time
options.C: Because glaciers deposit rock at the surface
options.D: Because temperature is measured at the seafloor

questions.explanation: The passage states that "because the layers accumulate steadily, their depth corresponds to age."

questions.stem: What is the function of paragraph 2 in relation to paragraph 1?

options.A: It questions whether ice ages truly occurred
options.B: It repeats paragraph 1 using new examples
options.C: It gives the historical background of glaciers
options.D: It presents the solution to the dating problem raised in paragraph 1

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 raises the problem of dating ice ages, and paragraph 2 introduces sediment cores as "the breakthrough," presenting the solution.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about changes in Earth's orbit?

options.A: They occur suddenly and without warning
options.B: They are not strong enough on their own to cause large climate swings
options.C: They have no measurable effect on the glaciers
options.D: They were discovered before the sediment cores

questions.explanation: The passage states "the orbital changes alone are too faint to drive such large swings," so they cannot cause the swings by themselves.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what did surface clues such as scratched bedrock reveal?

options.A: The exact dates when each ice age began
options.B: The chemical temperature record of ancient oceans
options.C: That ice had advanced and retreated repeatedly
options.D: That orbital cycles control the climate

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says surface clues "revealed that ice had advanced and retreated repeatedly," though they gave no reliable clock.
